edge_hypothesis: EMA_9/EMA_21 crossovers were declared 'exhausted' in Entry 001, but that conclusion was drawn from low-R:R (2-3:1) configurations. This spec tests the rehabilitation hypothesis: can the simplest trend signal produce ACCEPT-tier results when paired with extreme R:R (8:1)? The crossover itself is a fast-response trend initiation signal. At 2:1 R:R, whipsaws destroy PnL because losers are expensive relative to winners. At 8:1 R:R with 2.0 ATR stops, the crossover becomes a 'lottery ticket' — most trades stop out cheaply, but winners run 16x ATR before TP. If ETH 4h trends persist (as the Vortex and Supertrend ACCEPTs suggest), then even a 15-20% win rate produces positive expectancy. This is a meta-test: does R:R override signal quality as the dominant performance driver?

variants.label: EMA crossover 8:1 rehabilitation
entry_long: EMA_9 crosses above EMA_21 signaling bullish trend initiation
entry_short: EMA_9 crosses below EMA_21 signaling bearish trend initiation
exit_rules: Exit on ATR TP hit (16.0x ATR)
Exit on ATR stop hit (2.0x ATR)
Exit on opposite EMA crossover signal

variants.label: EMA crossover tight 10:1
entry_long: EMA_9 crosses above EMA_21 signaling bullish trend initiation
entry_short: EMA_9 crosses below EMA_21 signaling bearish trend initiation
exit_rules: Exit on ATR TP hit (15.0x ATR)
Exit on ATR stop hit (1.5x ATR)
Exit on opposite EMA crossover signal
risk_rules: Stop: 1.5x ATR (tight — tests whether tighter stop improves expectancy by reducing loss size despite more stopouts)

variants.label: EMA crossover moderate 6:1
entry_long: EMA_9 crosses above EMA_21 signaling bullish trend initiation
entry_short: EMA_9 crosses below EMA_21 signaling bearish trend initiation
exit_rules: Exit on ATR TP hit (15.0x ATR)
Exit on ATR stop hit (2.5x ATR)
Exit on opposite EMA crossover signal
risk_rules: Stop: 2.5x ATR (wider cushion — tests whether surviving more whipsaws increases win rate enough to offset smaller R:R)
